1: wayyērāʾ ʾēlāyw YHWH bĕʾēlōnê mamrēʾ — "YHWH appeared to him at the oaks of Mamre." The Niphal of rāʾāh (to see) → nĕraʾeh = "was seen/appeared" is the standard theophany formula throughout the patriarchal narratives (Genesis 12:7; Genesis 17:1; Genesis 26:2; Genesis 35:9; Exodus 3:2,16). The narrator opens by identifying the visitors as YHWH's appearance; the ambiguity that follows (three men, one speaks as YHWH, two go to Sodom as angels) is intentional — the text invites the reader to see the divine identity in the human form. Yōšēḇ peteḥ-hāʾōhel kĕḥōm hayyôm — "sitting at the entrance of the tent in the heat of the day" — the temporal setting (midday rest) makes the urgency of Abraham's hospitality response more vivid: he runs at noon to meet strangers.
2: šĕlōšāh ʾănāšîm niṣṣāḇîm ʿālāyw — "three men standing near him." The word ʾănāšîm (men, human beings) is the narrator's description — 19:1 will identify two of them as malākim (angels/messengers). The text moves between the two designations without resolving the tension explicitly. Wayyāroṣ liqrāʾtām mippeṯaḥ hāʾōhel — "he ran to meet them from the entrance of the tent" — the running (rûṣ) and the subsequent series of actions (hurrying, running, hastening) create a picture of extravagant, urgent hospitality that the NT epistle to the Hebrews alludes to: "Do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares" (Hebrews 13:2).
3: ʾăḏōnāy ʾim-nāʾ māṣāʾtî ḥēn bĕʿêneyḵā — "My Lord, if I have found favor in your eyes." The form ʾăḏōnāy — the pointed form with the special qames vowels — is the form traditionally reserved for the divine title (in distinction from ʾăḏōnî, "my lord," used of human superiors). Whether Abraham is addressing all three visitors or specifically the divine figure is debated; the use of ʾăḏōnāy suggests at least partial recognition of divine presence. Ḥēn (H2580, grace/favor) — to "find favor in the eyes of" is the standard idiom for receiving gracious attention from a superior (Noah in Genesis 6:8; Joseph in Genesis 39:4; the formula recurs throughout the Pentateuch). This may be the earliest use of the phrase in an explicitly theological register — Abraham appeals to YHWH's grace as the ground of his request for the divine presence to remain.
4: yuqqaḥ-nāʾ mĕʿaṭ-mayim wĕraḥăṣû raglêkem — "Let a little water be brought, and wash your feet." The foot-washing gesture in the ANE signaled a transition from the dusty road to domestic hospitality — the guest was received as a member of the household for the duration of the stay (cf. Luke 7:44; John 13:5). Abraham's word mĕʿaṭ ("a little") before "water" and pat-leḥem ("a morsel of bread") in v5 is deliberate understatement — what follows is three seahs of flour, a choice calf, curds, and milk. The understatement of offer followed by extravagant provision is a hospitality convention that signals social grace.
5: kî-ʿal-kēn ʿăḇartem ʿal-ʿaḇdĕkem — "since you have passed by your servant." The phrase ʿāḇar ʿal — "pass over/by" — when combined with the hospitality offer means "since you have condescended to come to your servant." The motive clause ("since you have come to me") grounds the hospitality in a recognition that the visitors' coming is itself a privilege, not a burden. ʾAḥar taʿăḇōrû — "and then you may go on your way" — the sequence is: wash, rest, eat, then proceed. The hospitality is not designed to delay or detain permanently but to honor the transition.
6: šĕlōš sĕʾîm qemet sōlet — "three seahs of fine flour." A seʾāh is approximately 7-8 liters; three seahs = roughly 22 liters of flour — enough for a significant feast, not a "morsel." The word sōlet (fine flour) is the word used in Leviticus for the finest grade of flour in grain offerings — the best quality. Ûlōšî wĕʿăśî ʿuggôt — "knead and make cakes." ʿUggôt (round cakes cooked on coals or in ashes, as in 1 Kings 19:6) — a quick bread for immediate hospitality. The urgency of preparation matches the urgency of the welcome.
7: wĕʾel-habbāqār rāṣ ʾaḇrāhām wayyiqqaḥ ben-bāqār raḵ wāṭôḇ — "And to the herd Abraham ran and took a calf, tender and good." Again the verb rûṣ (to run) — Abraham is running, not walking, throughout the hospitality scene. The calf is described as raḵ wāṭôḇ — "tender and good" — a double quality specification meaning the best of the herd. He gives the calf to a servant who "hastened" (waymahēr) to prepare it — the urgency cascades down: master runs, servant hurries. The narrative's repeated emphasis on speed creates a picture of extravagant, urgent generosity.
8: wayyiqqaḥ ḥemʾāh wĕḥālāḇ ûben-habbāqār ʾăšer ʿāśāh wayyittēn lipnêhem wĕhûʾ-ʿōmēḏ ʿălêhem taḥat hāʿēṣ wayyōʾkēlû — "He took curds and milk and the calf that had been prepared, and set it before them. And he stood beside them under the tree while they ate." The menu — curds, milk, veal — is a genuine meal, not a symbolic gesture. Importantly, "they ate" (wayyōʾkēlû) — the narrative does not retreat from the physical reality of divine messengers eating; this is not a visionary meal. The narrator uses this to establish genuine presence. Abraham stands while they eat — the posture of a host, not a dining companion; he does not eat with them but serves. The scene of the divine eating in Abraham's tent is the background for the Emmaus meal (Luke 24:30-31) and the promise of eating with Christ (Revelation 3:20).
9: ʾayyēh śārāh ʾišĕtĕḵā — "Where is Sarah your wife?" The question comes from the divine visitors who already know the answer — it is a question designed to draw Sarah into the encounter and to set up the announcement. The inquiry into Sarah's whereabouts parallels God's question to Adam ("Where are you?" Genesis 3:9) and to Cain ("Where is Abel your brother?" Genesis 4:9) — a divine question that is never a request for information but a summons to attention and response. Hinnēh bāʾōhel — "There, in the tent" — Abraham's answer simply locates her; the tent will become the site from which she hears the promise that changes everything.
10: šôḇ ʾāšûḇ ʾēleyḵā kāʿēt ḥayyāh — "I will surely return to you about this time next year." The Qal infinitive absolute + imperfect (šôḇ ʾāšûḇ) creates the emphatic certain-future: "I will certainly, without fail, return." This construction appears in the covenant promise contexts to stress absolute commitment (cf. Genesis 22:17; Genesis 26:28). Kāʿēt ḥayyāh — "at the living time" or "when the time revives" — a temporal idiom that means "at this time next year" (cf. 2 Kings 4:16,17 of Elisha's promise to the Shunammite woman; both cases use the exact phrase). The speaker declares his specific intention to return — the personal return of the divine visitor is part of the promise.
11: wĕśārāh šāmĕʿāh peteḥ hāʾōhel wĕhûʾ ʾaḥărāyw — "Sarah was listening at the entrance of the tent behind him." The participle šōmaʿat (listening) captures her real-time reception of the conversation. The physical arrangement — she is at the tent entrance, behind Abraham — places her as overhearing what is spoken to Abraham. Wĕʾaḇrāhām wĕśārāh zĕqēnîm — "Abraham and Sarah were old, advanced in years" — the double qualification (zĕqēnîm and bāʾîm bayyāmîm) stresses the magnitude of the obstacle. Ḥādal lihyôt lĕśārāh ʾōraḥ kannāšîm — "the way of women had ceased for Sarah" — a circumlocution for menopause; the narrator is unambiguous that human reproduction is biologically impossible.
12: wattiṣḥaq śārāh bĕqirbiāh — "Sarah laughed to herself." The verb ṣāḥaq — the same root as Isaac's name — appears again (cf. Abraham's laughter in Genesis 17:17). Sarah's laughter is explicitly internal (bĕqirbiāh, within herself), but its content is quoted directly by God in v13 — YHWH has access to what is said within the heart. ʾAḥarê bĕlōtî — "after I have worn out" — bālāh (H1086) = to wear out, consume; used of old garments wearing out in Isaiah 51:6 and Deuteronomy 8:4; Genesis 29:5. Sarah's language is bleakly physical: her body has worn out. Wĕʾăḏōnî zāqēn — "and my lord is old" — Sarah's term for Abraham, ʾăḏōnî ("my lord"), reflects the household authority structure and is noted approvingly in 1 Peter 3:6.
13: lāmmāh zeh ṣāḥăqāh śārāh lēʾmōr — "Why did Sarah laugh, saying..." YHWH's question reveals omniscience: he heard the internal laughter that was not audible. The challenge is not punitive but corrective — making Sarah aware that her internal response has been heard and must be addressed. The question "Why?" invites Sarah to acknowledge the faith-failure rather than condemning her outright. The omniscience of YHWH over the interior life of the human heart (the lēḇ) is consistent throughout Scripture (Psalms 139:1-4; 1 Samuel 16:7; 1 Kings 8:39; Jeremiah 17:10; Revelation 2:23).
14: hăyippālēʾ mēYHWH dāḇār — "Is anything too hard for YHWH?" The verb pālāʾ (H6381) — to be wonderful, extraordinary, surpassing — is used of divine acts that exceed human capacity: the plagues (Psalms 78:12), divine love (Psalms 139:6), and the divine name (Judges 13:18). The same root gives the noun peleʾ (wonder, miracle) used in Isaiah 9:6 as a name of the Messiah. The rhetorical question expects the answer "nothing" — and the form is repeated almost verbatim in Jeremiah 32:27: "Behold, I am YHWH, the God of all flesh; is anything too hard for me?" Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:37) echoes this: "For nothing will be impossible with God." Lammôʿēḏ — "at the appointed time" — môʿēḏ (assembly, appointed season) is the same word used for the festival calendar; the birth of Isaac will be on schedule according to God's appointed time.
15: wayyōʾmer lōʾ kî ṣāḥāqt — "He said, 'No, but you did laugh.'" YHWH's two-word correction (lōʾ kî ṣāḥāqt) refuses the denial and asserts the truth of what he heard. The flat denial followed by the unambiguous correction establishes that YHWH cannot be misled by face-saving denial. The narrative does not record further dialogue — the correction stands, Sarah does not argue again, and the encounter moves on. The absence of further rebuke or punishment suggests that the correction is complete in the naming of the truth: she laughed, she is told she laughed, the son to be born will bear the name of that laughter.
16: wayyāšuqpû miššām ʿal-pĕnê sĕḏom — "they looked toward Sodom from there." The verb šāqap (to look down upon, overlook) is used in vv16 and 28 — Abraham also "looks down" (wayyaṣṣēp) toward Sodom from Mamre in v28 after the destruction. The geographic vantage point (the hills of Hebron-Mamre overlook the Jordan Valley and the Dead Sea region) is accurate. Wĕʾaḇrāhām hōlēk ʿimmām lĕšalĕḥām — "and Abraham walked with them to send them off" — the verb šilaḥ (to send, dismiss) is the customary gesture of completed hospitality; the host escorts the guests to the road.
17: hămekaséh ʾănî mēʾaḇrāhām ʾăšer ʾănî ʿōśeh — "Shall I hide from Abraham what I am about to do?" The divine deliberation is cast in the form of an internal divine question — hămekaséh (the interrogative + Piel infinitive of kāsāh, to cover, hide). The Piel of kāsāh = to cover/conceal; the question is whether YHWH's covenant relationship with Abraham creates an obligation of disclosure. The same principle is stated in Amos 3:7: "Surely the Lord GOD does nothing without revealing his secret (sôdô) to his servants the prophets." The divine "deliberation" is a narrative device that establishes Abraham as a covenant partner, not merely a covenant recipient.
18: wĕniḇrĕḵû bô kol gôyê hāʾāreṣ — "and all nations of the earth shall bless themselves by him" (or: "shall be blessed through him"). The verb bāraḵ in the Niphal can be passive ("shall be blessed") or reflexive ("shall bless themselves by/in his name"). The same promise formula appears in 12:3 and Genesis 22:18 (where the Hithpael form is used, explicitly reflexive). Paul cites Genesis 12:3 and 18:18 in Galatians 3:8-9 as the "gospel announced beforehand," reading the promise as a passive blessing flowing from Christ to the nations. The LXX uses eneulogēthēsontai (will be blessed), supporting the passive reading that the NT adopts.
19: kî yĕdaʿtîw lĕmaʿan ʾăšer yĕṣawweh ʾet-bānāyw — "For I have known/chosen him, so that he will command his children." The verb yāḏaʿ (H3045) in the Qal perfect first person — "I have known him" — carries the full covenantal-elective sense: personal, relational, purposive knowing. Amos 3:2 uses the same construction: "You only have I known of all the families of the earth." The word goes beyond cognition to encompass election, commitment, and relationship. Lĕmaʿan ("so that/in order that") — the purpose clause makes the election teleological: YHWH chose Abraham so that Abraham would form a household ordered by covenant faithfulness — not for Abraham's own sake alone but for the formation of a community of justice and righteousness (ṣĕdāqāh ûmišpāṭ). The pairing ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) and mišpāṭ (justice) is the characteristic Amos-Isaiah-Micah formula for covenant social ethics (Amos 5:24; Isaiah 1:17; Micah 6:8).
20: zaʿăqat sĕḏōm waʿămōrāh kî-rāḇāh — "The cry of Sodom and Gomorrah — because it is great." Zaʿaqāh (H2201, outcry, cry for help) — the same root as the cry of the oppressed in Egypt (Exodus 2:23; Genesis 3:7,9). The "cry" that rises to God is typically the cry of those wronged and powerless — here it is the cry of Sodom's victims (Ezekiel 16:49-50 identifies the sin as pride, excess food, prosperous ease, and failure to help the poor and needy, plus the act of Genesis 19:5). Wĕḥaṭṭāʾtām kî-kāḇĕḏāh mĕʾōḏ — "and their sin, because it is very heavy" — kāḇēḏ (heavy, weighty) is the same word for Pharaoh's "heavy" heart in Exodus. Sin as weight or burden pressing on the relationship is a consistent metaphor.
21: ʾērĕḏāh-nnāʾ wĕʾerʾeh — "I will go down and see." YHWH announces a divine descent to investigate — a deliberate anthropomorphism that echoes Genesis 11:5 (God descending to see the tower of Babel) and Genesis 11:7 ("Let us go down and there confuse their language"). The descent language is part of the divine-judge metaphor: a just judge investigates before pronouncing verdict. Hăkiṣṣaʿăqātiāh bāʾāh ʾēlay ʿāśû kālāh wĕʾim-lōʾ ʾēḏāʿāh — "whether they have done altogether according to the outcry that has come to me; and if not, I will know." The conditional ("and if not, I will know") establishes that the judgment is not predetermined — YHWH investigates; the outcome depends on the investigation. This is the grammar of divine justice: heard evidence, investigation, verdict.
22: wĕhāʾănāšîm pānû miššām wayyēlĕḵû sĕḏōmāh wĕʾaḇrāhām ʿôḏennû ʿōmēḏ lipnê YHWH — "the men turned from there and went toward Sodom, but Abraham was still standing before YHWH." The contrast is sharp: the angels depart on their mission, but Abraham remains. ʿōmēḏ lipnê YHWH — "standing before YHWH" — the posture of a court official, petitioner, or intercessor. The phrase is used of prophetic and priestly ministry (Deuteronomy 10:8; 1 Kings 17:1; Jeremiah 15:1). Abraham's standing before YHWH is the posture of prayer about to be articulated in vv23-32.
23: wayyiggaš ʾaḇrāhām wayyōʾmar — "Abraham drew near and said." The verb nāgaš (to draw near, approach) is used for priestly approach to the altar (Exodus 19:22; Genesis 28:43; Leviticus 10:3; Ezekiel 44:13-16) and for judicial approach — the advocate drawing near to plead a case. Abraham's approach to YHWH over Sodom is an act of intercession that positions him as both priest and advocate. Hăʾap tissĕpeh ṣaddîq ʿim-rāšāʿ — "Will you sweep away the righteous with the wicked?" Sāpāh (to sweep away) — the same word used in v24 for the whole place being swept away. The question is a legal one: does collective judgment honor the principle that each person is accountable for their own sin (Ezekiel 18:20; Deuteronomy 24:16)?
24: ʾûlay yēš ḥămišîm ṣaddîqim bĕtôk hāʿîr — "What if there are fifty righteous people in the city?" The term ṣaddîq (H6662, righteous) — from the root ṣādaq (to be right/straight/just) — is the standard term for the morally and legally upright person. The opening bid of fifty provides the starting point for the negotiation. The theological principle Abraham applies: the presence of the righteous creates a claim on God's mercy for the community they inhabit. This principle works the other way in Amos 5:3 (where too few righteous remain) and positively in Isaiah 37:35 (God defends Jerusalem for David's sake). The logic of corporate benefit from the righteous few underlies 1 Peter 2:9.
25: ḥālîlāh lĕḵā mēʿăśōt kaddāḇār hazzeh lĕhāmît ṣaddîq ʿim-rāšāʿ — "Far be it from you to do such a thing — to kill the righteous with the wicked." Ḥālîlāh (H2486) — an exclamation of moral repudiation, literally "profanation (be it from you)!" It occurs predominantly in the mouth of patriarchs and in prophetic rejection of unthinkable acts (Genesis 44:7,17; Joshua 22:29; Genesis 24:16; 1 Samuel 12:23; Genesis 14:45; Genesis 20:2; Job 27:5). Hăšōpēṭ kol-hāʾāreṣ lōʾ yaʿăśeh mišpāṭ — "Shall not the Judge of all the earth do justice?" The epithet "Judge of all the earth" (šōpēṭ kol-hāʾāreṣ) makes the argument from YHWH's universal judicial character: justice is not just a preference but the defining attribute of the one who governs all peoples. The same attribute grounds the Psalms' confidence in divine vindication (Psalms 7:8; Genesis 9:4,8; Genesis 50:6).
26: ʾim-ʾemṣāʾ biṣḏōm ḥămišîm ṣaddîqim bĕtôk hāʿîr wĕnāśāʾtî lĕkol-hamāqôm biʿăḇûrām — "If I find in Sodom fifty righteous within the city, I will spare the whole place for their sake." Nāśāʾ (to lift up, bear, forgive/spare) — the same verb used for bearing sin in the formula of absolution (Numbers 14:18-19; Psalms 32:1,5; Isaiah 33:24; Micah 7:18). God will "bear" or "lift up" the city — the forensic acquittal of the whole based on the merit of the righteous few. Biʿăḇûrām — "for their sake" — the preposition bĕ + ʿāḇar (for the sake of, on account of): the righteous serve as the ground of the community's preservation.
27: hinnēh-nāʾ hôʾaltî lĕdabbēr ʾel-ʾăḏōnāy wĕʾānōḵî ʿāpār wāʾēper — "Behold, I have undertaken to speak to the Lord, though I am dust and ashes." Abraham's self-description ʿāpār wāʾēper ("dust and ashes") combines two terms for low estate and mortality: ʿāpār (dust, as in Genesis 2:7, Genesis 3:19 — the substance of human mortality) and ʾēper (ashes, the remains of burning — cf. Job 30:19; Genesis 42:6). The doubled humility formula frames the intercession as an act of audacious grace-dependence: Abraham knows he has no standing to argue with the divine judge, yet he proceeds because he has been given access. This combination is the foundation of prophetic and priestly intercession in the Hebrew tradition.
28: ʾûlay yaḥsĕrûn ḥămišîm haṣṣaddîqim ḥămiššāh — "What if the fifty righteous are lacking five?" The negotiation proceeds in decrements: 50→45→40→30→20→10. The descending series follows a pattern of pressing the divine generosity: each successful reduction reveals that God's willingness to spare is not merely proportional arithmetic but reflects a deep reluctance to destroy. The word ḥāśēr (to lack, be deficient) — "What if they are five short?" — frames the argument as whether God will be rigidly exact or generously rounding. The answer is always generous: lōʾ ʾašḥît, "I will not destroy."
29: ʾûlay yimmāṣĕʾûn šām ʾarbāʿîm wayyōʾmer lōʾ ʾeʿĕśeh biʿăḇûr hāʾarbāʿîm — "What if forty are found there? He said, I will not do it for the sake of forty." The pattern of intercession — question / divine concession — repeats five times (vv28-32). Each exchange follows the same grammar: Abraham proposes a hypothetical (ʾûlay, "what if?"); YHWH responds with the preservation formula. The consistent divine response shows that YHWH is not reluctant to spare — he is willing at every threshold Abraham names. The negotiation reveals not a God who must be argued into mercy but a God whose mercy is deeper than the questioner's asking.
30: ʾal-nāʾ yiḥar lāʾḏōnāy wăʾădabbērāh — "Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak." Abraham's repeated apology for the audacity of further pressing (ʾal-nāʾ yiḥar — "let it not burn/be hot to the Lord") appears in vv30 and 32. The verb ḥārāh (H2734, to be hot/angry) — the idiom "it was hot to someone" = they were angered. Abraham's hedging of each new proposal with this apology marks the social asymmetry of the encounter: he is a petitioner before a sovereign, and each new request requires the acknowledgment of potential impertinence. That YHWH never shows anger validates the bold approach.
31: hinnēh-nāʾ hôʾaltî lĕdabbēr ʾel-ʾăḏōnāy ʾûlay yimmāṣĕʾûn šām ʿeśrîm — "Behold, I have undertaken to speak to the Lord — what if twenty are found there?" The fifth round of intercession. The verb hôʾîl (Hiphil of yaʾal, to undertake, be willing, presume to) — Abraham keeps acknowledging the audacity of each new step. The decrements show that Abraham is pressing toward the lower limit of what he thinks might actually be present in Sodom — ten may be the number he estimates for Lot's household (Lot, his wife, his daughters, their pledged husbands, and any other dependents). The intercession is not abstract but aimed at saving specific people.
32: ʾal-nāʾ yiḥar lāʾḏōnāy wăʾădabbĕrāh ʾaḵ-happaʿam ʾûlay yimmāṣĕʾûn šām ʿăśārāh — "Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak just this once more — what if ten are found?" The final threshold: ten righteous. Ten is the minimum viable social unit in later rabbinic tradition (the minyan required for communal prayer) — likely reflecting this very passage. Abraham stops at ten, presumably because he cannot argue below the minimum that would constitute a community. That even ten are not found (only Lot and his family are extracted, and the sons-in-law refuse to flee) shows the depth of Sodom's corruption. The intercession is not in vain — Lot is saved — but the city falls.
33: wayyēlek YHWH kaʾăšer killāh lĕdabbēr ʾel-ʾaḇrāhām wĕʾaḇrāhām šāḇ limĕqōmô — "YHWH went when he had finished speaking to Abraham, and Abraham returned to his place." The narrative closes the intercession with a symmetrical departure: YHWH leaves, Abraham returns. The word killāh (Piel perfect of kālāh, to complete, finish) — when YHWH had "finished" — the conversation had a natural close; God did not end it abruptly. Wayyāšāḇ limĕqōmô — "he returned to his place" — Abraham goes back to Mamre; the intercession is complete and Abraham can only wait. The juxtaposition of the divine departure and Abraham's return marks the end of the mediatorial encounter that governed Genesis 18:16-33.
